items.myth: Tags and experiment IDs are interchangeable.
items.reality: IDs are unique per run; tags are repeatable labels for grouping. You need the ID to fetch one run exactly; tags filter many runs.
items.example: Ten runs can share tag `lr-sweep`, but each has a distinct ID like `a3f91c2` for precise artifact retrieval.

items.myth: The model registry stores all training history automatically.
items.reality: Experiment tracking stores all runs; the registry holds only models you explicitly register and version for promotion.
items.example: 100 sweep runs stay in the experiment log; only run `7b2e10` becomes `fraud-detector` version 5 in the registry.

items.myth: Highest metric on the leaderboard is always safe to promote.
items.reality: Comparison must account for data leakage, eval split, seed luck, and preprocessing version logged on each run ID.
items.example: Run `x9aa01` tops val_f1 but used test data in training—tag `invalid-split` and exclude it from comparison.

items.myth: Version numbers in filenames are enough without a registry.
items.reality: Ad hoc `model_v3.pkl` files lose linkage to hyperparameters and metrics unless tied to a tracked run ID.
items.example: `model_v3.pkl` on disk without run ID cannot answer which learning rate trained it; registry version 3 stores that link.
